1173	\subsection{Regularity at arbitrary primes (Serre-free Stacks 00TT)}
1174	\label{subsec:smooth_prime_regularity}
1175	
1176	The closed-point regularity of \cref{lem:standard_smooth_regular_at_closed_point_algclosed}
1177	extends to \emph{every} prime of a standard-smooth algebra over a perfect
1178	field, without invoking the localisation theorem for regular local rings
1179	(Stacks tag 00OF, Serre's homological characterisation). The route combines a
1180	transcendence-degree/height inequality in polynomial rings with the conormal
1181	sequence at an arbitrary prime.
1182	
1183	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1184	[One-variable step of the trdeg--height inequality]
1185	  \label{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg}
1186	  \lean{Polynomial.step_height_trdeg_of_isPrime}
1187	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00ON (dimension formula for going-down
1188	  % extensions, Matsumura 13.B Theorem 19(2)), used through Mathlib's
1189	  % Ideal.height_eq_height_add_of_liesOver_of_hasGoingDown.
1190	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(R\) a Noetherian \(k\)-algebra, \(P \subseteq R[X]\)
1191	  a prime ideal, and \(p = P \cap R\). Then either
1192	  \[\operatorname{ht} p + 1 \le \operatorname{ht} P
1193	    \quad\text{and}\quad
1194	    \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p) \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P),\]
1195	  or
1196	  \[\operatorname{ht} P = \operatorname{ht} p
1197	    \quad\text{and}\quad
1198	    \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p) + 1 \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P).\]
1199	\end{lemma}
1200	
1201	\begin{proof}
1202	  \leanok
1203	  The extension \(R \to R[X]\) is free, hence flat, hence satisfies going-down,
1204	  so the dimension formula (Stacks 00ON) gives
1205	  \(\operatorname{ht} P = \operatorname{ht} p + \operatorname{ht} \bar P\) for
1206	  the image \(\bar P\) of \(P\) in \(R[X]/pR[X] \cong (R/p)[X]\). Write
1207	  \(A = R/p\) (a domain) and \(Q \subseteq A[X]\) for the prime corresponding
1208	  to \(\bar P\); then \(Q \cap A = 0\) and \(A[X]/Q \cong R[X]/P\) as
1209	  \(k\)-algebras. If \(Q = 0\), the heights agree and
1210	  \(R[X]/P \cong A[X]\); superadditivity of transcendence degree in the tower
1211	  \(k \to A \to A[X]\) together with \(\operatorname{trdeg}_A A[X] = 1\) gives
1212	  \(\operatorname{trdeg}_k A[X] \ge \operatorname{trdeg}_k A + 1\). If
1213	  \(Q \neq 0\), then \(0 \subsetneq Q\) is a chain of primes in the domain
1214	  \(A[X]\), so \(\operatorname{ht} \bar P = \operatorname{ht} Q \ge 1\), while
1215	  \(A\) still embeds into \(A[X]/Q\) because \(Q \cap A = 0\), whence
1216	  \(\operatorname{trdeg}_k A \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (A[X]/Q)\).
1217	\end{proof}
1218	
1219	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1220	[trdeg--height inequality at primes of polynomial rings]
1221	  \label{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height}
1222	  \lean{MvPolynomial.exists_le_trdeg_and_natCard_le_height_add}
1223	  \uses{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg}
1224	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(\iota\) a finite set, and
1225	  \(P \subseteq k[x_i : i \in \iota]\) a prime ideal. Then there is
1226	  \(d \in \mathbb N\) with
1227	  \[d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k \bigl(k[x_i]/P\bigr)
1228	    \qquad\text{and}\qquad
1229	    \#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} P + d.\]
1230	  (Classically \(d\) may be taken to be the transcendence degree of the residue
1231	  field and both inequalities are equalities; the witness form suffices for the
1232	  regularity pipeline and requires no Noether normalisation.)
1233	\end{lemma}
1234	
1235	\begin{proof}
1236	  \leanok
1237	  Induction on \(\#\iota\). If \(\iota = \emptyset\) take \(d = 0\). For the
1238	  step, write \(k[x_i : i \in \iota] = R[X]\) with
1239	  \(R = k[x_i : i \in \iota\setminus\{i_0\}]\) and contract \(P\) to
1240	  \(p = P \cap R\); the inductive hypothesis provides \(d\) with
1241	  \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R/p)\) and \(\#\iota - 1 \le
1242	  \operatorname{ht} p + d\). In the height-jump case of
1243	  \cref{lem:polynomial_step_height_trdeg},
1244	  \(\#\iota \le (\operatorname{ht} p + 1) + d \le \operatorname{ht} P + d\)
1245	  and the same \(d\) works; in the height-preserving case,
1246	  \(\#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} P + (d+1)\) and
1247	  \(d + 1 \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (R[X]/P)\), so \(d+1\) works.
1248	\end{proof}
1249	
1250	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1251	[trdeg--height inequality for standard-smooth algebras]
1252	  \label{lem:standard_smooth_trdeg_height}
1253	  \lean{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.exists_le_trdeg_and_natCast_le_height_add}
1254	  \uses{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height}
1255	  Let \(S\) be a standard-smooth algebra of relative dimension \(n\) over a
1256	  field \(k\) and \(q \subseteq S\) a prime ideal. Then there is
1257	  \(d \in \mathbb N\) with \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q)\) and
1258	  \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\).
1259	\end{lemma}
1260	
1261	\begin{proof}
1262	  \leanok
1263	  Choose a submersive presentation \(k[x_i : i \in \iota] \twoheadrightarrow S\)
1264	  whose kernel is generated by \(\#\sigma\) relations, with
1265	  \(n = \#\iota - \#\sigma\). Let \(M\) be the preimage of \(q\); then
1266	  \(k[x_i]/M \cong S/q\), and \cref{lem:mvpoly_trdeg_height} provides \(d\)
1267	  with \(d \le \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q)\) and
1268	  \(\#\iota \le \operatorname{ht} M + d\). The relative form of Krull's height
1269	  theorem bounds \(\operatorname{ht} M \le \operatorname{ht} q + \#\sigma\)
1270	  (the same argument as in \cref{lem:standard_smooth_le_height_of_isMaximal});
1271	  cancelling \(\#\sigma\) gives \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\).
1272	\end{proof}
1273	
1274	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1275	[K\"ahler rank equals transcendence degree over a perfect field]
1276	  \label{lem:kaehler_rank_eq_trdeg}
1277	  \lean{Algebra.rank_kaehlerDifferential_eq_trdeg}
1278	  Let \(k\) be a perfect field and \(K\) a field extension of \(k\) that is
1279	  essentially of finite type. Then
1280	  \[\dim_K \Omega_{K/k} = \operatorname{trdeg}_k K.\]
1281	\end{lemma}
1282	
1283	\begin{proof}
1284	  \leanok
1285	  Since \(k\) is perfect, \(K\) admits a separating transcendence basis \(s\):
1286	  writing \(F = k(s)\) for the rational-function subfield, the extension
1287	  \(K/F\) is separable algebraic, hence formally \'etale, so base change along
1288	  \(F \to K\) identifies \(\Omega_{K/k} \cong K \otimes_F \Omega_{F/k}\).
1289	  Moreover \(F\) is the fraction field of the polynomial ring \(k[s]\), so
1290	  \(\Omega_{F/k}\) is the localisation of \(\Omega_{k[s]/k}\), which is free
1291	  with basis \(\{dx_i : i \in s\}\). Hence
1292	  \(\dim_K \Omega_{K/k} = \#s = \operatorname{trdeg}_k K\).
1293	\end{proof}
1294	
1295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1296	[Conormal dimension identity at an arbitrary prime]
1297	  \label{lem:conormal_finrank_identity}
1298	  \lean{finrank_cotangentSpace_add_finrank_kaehler_residueField}
1299	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02JK (the conormal sequence of a formally
1300	  % smooth local algebra).
1301	  Let \((S_{\mathfrak m}, \mathfrak m, \kappa)\) be a local \(R\)-algebra that
1302	  is formally smooth over \(R\), whose residue field \(\kappa\) is also
1303	  formally smooth over \(R\), and whose K\"ahler module
1304	  \(\Omega_{S_{\mathfrak m}/R}\) is free of rank \(n\). Then
1305	  \[\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 + \dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/R} = n.\]
1306	\end{lemma}
1307	
1308	\begin{proof}
1309	  \leanok
1310	  The conormal sequence
1311	  \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \to \kappa \otimes_{S_{\mathfrak m}}
1312	  \Omega_{S_{\mathfrak m}/R} \to \Omega_{\kappa/R} \to 0\)
1313	  is exact, and formal smoothness of \(\kappa\) over \(R\) provides a
1314	  retraction of its first map, which is therefore (split) injective. The
1315	  middle term has \(\kappa\)-dimension \(n\) by freeness, and the last map is
1316	  surjective; rank--nullity gives the identity.
1317	\end{proof}

1319	\begin{theorem}\leanok
1320	[Standard-smooth algebras over a perfect field are regular at every prime]
1321	  \label{thm:standard_smooth_regular_at_prime}
1322	  \lean{isRegularLocalRing_of_isLocalization_atPrime_of_isStandardSmooth_of_perfectField}
1323	  \uses{lem:standard_smooth_trdeg_height, lem:kaehler_rank_eq_trdeg,
1324	        lem:conormal_finrank_identity, lem:regular_of_finrank_cotangent_le_dim,
1325	        lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime,
1326	        lem:module_free_kaehler_localization,
1327	        lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
1328	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT (smooth algebras over fields), proved
1329	  % here without tag 00OF (localisation of regular local rings).
1330	  Let \(k\) be a perfect field, \(S\) a standard-smooth \(k\)-algebra of
1331	  relative dimension \(n\), and \(q \subseteq S\) any prime ideal. Then any
1332	  localisation \(S_q\) of \(S\) at \(q\) is a regular local ring.
1333	\end{theorem}
1334	
1335	\begin{proof}
1336	  \leanok
1337	  Write \(\kappa\) for the residue field of \(S_q\). The K\"ahler module
1338	  \(\Omega_{S_q/k}\) is free of rank \(n\) (the localisation of the free
1339	  module \(\Omega_{S/k}\), \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization} and
1340	  \cref{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}); \(S_q\) is formally
1341	  smooth over \(k\) (localisations of standard-smooth algebras are formally
1342	  smooth), and \(\kappa\) is formally smooth over \(k\) since it is an
1343	  essentially-finite-type field extension of a perfect field (separating
1344	  transcendence bases exist). By \cref{lem:conormal_finrank_identity},
1345	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 = n - \dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/k}\).
1346	  By \cref{lem:kaehler_rank_eq_trdeg} and the embedding of \(S/q\) into
1347	  \(\kappa\), \(\dim_\kappa \Omega_{\kappa/k} = \operatorname{trdeg}_k \kappa
1348	  \ge \operatorname{trdeg}_k (S/q) \ge d\) for the witness \(d\) of
1349	  \cref{lem:standard_smooth_trdeg_height}, which also gives
1350	  \(n \le \operatorname{ht} q + d\). Hence
1351	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 \le n - d \le \operatorname{ht} q
1352	  = \dim S_q\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDim_localization_eq_height_atPrime}), and a
1353	  Noetherian local ring whose cotangent dimension is at most its Krull
1354	  dimension is regular (\cref{lem:regular_of_finrank_cotangent_le_dim}).
1355	\end{proof}

1461	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
1462	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
1463	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
1464	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
1465	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
1466	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
1467	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
1468	
1469	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
1470	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
1471	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
1472	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
1473	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
1474	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
1475	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
1476	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
1477	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
1478	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
1479	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
1480	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
1481	\end{proof}
1482	
1483	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
1484	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
1485	
1486	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
1487	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
1488	point.
1489	
1490	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1491	[Milne Theorem~3.1, codim-\(\geq 2\) conclusion]
1492	  \label{thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2}
1493	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_codimGe2_of_smooth_of_complete}
1494	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
1495	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
1496	  % valuative-criterion half of the proof).
1497	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), \(Y\) a complete variety
1498	  over \(\bar k\), and \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) a rational map. Then
1499	  every point of the indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\)
1500	  (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has coheight at least \(2\); that is,
1501	  \(Z(f)\) contains no point of codimension \(\le 1\).
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	
1504	\begin{proof}
1505	  The generic point of \(X\) lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\), which is a dense
1506	  open, so no point of coheight \(0\) lies in \(Z(f)\). Suppose \(z \in Z(f)\)
1507	  had coheight \(1\). By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} the stalk
1508	  \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\) is a discrete valuation ring with fraction field the
1509	  function field \(K(X)\). The rational map \(f\) determines a morphism
1510	  \(\Spec K(X) \to Y\), and the valuative criterion of properness for the
1511	  complete variety \(Y\) extends it to a morphism
1512	  \(\Spec \mathcal O_{X,z} \to Y\). Spreading out, \(f\) admits a
1513	  representative defined on an open set containing \(z\), contradicting
1514	  \(z \in Z(f)\).
1515	\end{proof}
1516	
1517	
1518	\begin{theorem}
1519	
1520	
1521	\leanok
1522	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
1523	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
1524	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
1525	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim_one_indeterminacy,
1526	        thm:indeterminacy_codimGe2, cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1527	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
1528	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
1529	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
1530	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
1531	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
1532	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
1533	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
1534	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
1535	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
1536	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
1537	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1538	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
1539	
1540	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
1541	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
1542	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X
1543	  \to Y\). The extension is unique because \(X\) is reduced and \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\) is dense
1544	  in \(X\).
1545	\end{theorem}
1546	
1547	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
1548	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
1549	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
1550	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
1551	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
1552	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
1553	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
1554	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
1555	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
1556	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
1557	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
1558	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
1559	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
1560	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
1561	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
1562	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
1563	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
1564	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
1565	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
1566	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
1567	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
1568	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
1569	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
1570	% which is a contradiction."
1571	\begin{proof}
1572	  
1573	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
1574	
1575	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
1576	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
1577	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
1578	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
1579	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
1580	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
1581	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
1582	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
1583	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
1584	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
1585	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
1586	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
1587	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
1588	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular \(\eta \in
1589	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), contradicting \(\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\). Hence no codim-\(1\)
1590	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).

1592	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
1593	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
1594	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
1595	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
1596	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
1597	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
1598	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
1599	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
1600	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
1601	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
1602	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
1603	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
1604	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
1605	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
1606	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
1607	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
1608	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
1609	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
1610	
1611	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
1612	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
1613	  agree on all of \(X\).
1614	\end{proof}

1616	\begin{remark}
1617	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
1618	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
1619	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
1620	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
1621	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
1622	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
1623	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
1624	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
1625	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
1626	
1627	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
1628	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
1629	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
1630	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
1631	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
1632	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
1633	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
1634	\end{remark}

1636	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
1637	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
1638	
1639	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
1640	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
1641	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
1642	this forces the locus to be empty.
1643	
1644	\begin{lemma}
1645	
1646	
1647	\leanok
1648	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
1649	  variety]
1650	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
1651	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
1652	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point,
1653	        def:principal_divisor}
1654	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
1655	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
1656	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
1657	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
1658	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
1659	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
1660	  % divisors).}"
1661	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
1662	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
1663	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow
1664	  G\) be a rational map. Then either \(f\) is defined on all of \(X\), or the indeterminacy
1665	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
1666	  union of prime divisors).
1667	
1668	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
1669	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
1670	\end{lemma}
1671	
1672	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
1673	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
1674	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
1675	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
1676	% map represented by
1677	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
1678	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
1679	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
1680	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
1681	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
1682	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
1683	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
1684	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),

1687	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is

1694	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
1695	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
1696	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
1697	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
1698	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
1699	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
1700	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
1701	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
1702	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
1703	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
1704	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
1705	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
1706	\begin{proof}
1707	  
1708	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
1709	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
1710	
1711	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
1712	  \[
1713	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
1714	    \qquad
1715	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
1716	  \]
1717	  via the composition
1718	  \[
1719	    U \times U
1720	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
1721	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
1722	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
1723	  \]
1724	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
1725	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
1726	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
1727	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
1728	
1729	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
1730	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
1731	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
1732	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
1733	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
1734	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
1735	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
1736	  identity
1737	  \[
1738	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
1739	    \qquad u \in W',
1740	  \]
1741	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence \(x \in
1742	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\) if and only if \((x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\).
1743	
1744	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
1745	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
1746	  \(K(X \times X)\):
1747	  \[
1748	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
1749	  \]
1750	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
1751	  \((x, x)\) if and only if \(\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
1752	  \times X, (x, x)}\) (i.e.\ every local function on \(G\) near \(e\) has no pole at
1753	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
1754	
1755	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
1756	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
1757	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
1758	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
1759	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
1760	  \[
1761	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
1762	  \]
1763	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
1764	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
1765	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
1766	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
1767	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
1768	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
1769	  \[
1770	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
1771	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
1772	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
1773	  \]
1774	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
1775	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
1776	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
1777	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
1778	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
1779	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
1780	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
1781	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
1782	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
1783	
1784	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
1785	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some \(f_0 \in
1786	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\) with \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)\).
1787	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection \(\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
1788	  \Delta_X\) is pure codimension \(1\) in \(\Delta_X\), passing through \((x, x)\).
1789	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
1790	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
1791	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
1792	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
1793	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
1794	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
1795	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
1796	\end{proof}
1797	
1798	\begin{remark}
1799	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
1800	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
1801	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
1802	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
1803	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
1804	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
1805	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
1806	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
1807	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
1808	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
1809	\end{remark}
1810	
1811	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
1812	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
1813	
1814	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
1815	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
1816	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
1817	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
1818	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
1819	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
1820	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
1821	
1822	
1823	\begin{remark}
1824	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
1825	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
1826	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
1827	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
1828	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
1829	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
1830	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
1831	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
1832	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
1833	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
1834	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
1835	\end{remark}
1836	
1837	
1838	\begin{remark}
1839	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
1840	  \textbf{Relationship to \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
1841	  \texttt{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
1842	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
1843	  reformulation that \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
1844	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
1845	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
1846	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
1847	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
1848	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
1849	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
1850	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
1851	  history.
1852	\end{remark}
1853	
1854	\section{Lean encoding}
1855	\label{sec:codim1_lean_encoding}
1856	
1857	The Lean target file is the new
1858	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}. The declarations to
1859	introduce, in order:
1860	
1861	\begin{enumerate}
1862	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} ---
1863	    \cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}, a method on the Mathlib
1864	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure. Implementation: \texttt{X.carrier
1865	    \textbackslash{} f.domain} as a \texttt{Set X}, paired with an
1866	    \texttt{IsClosed} witness (either bundled or as a separate
1867	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus} lemma).
1868	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} ---
1869	    \cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}, a \texttt{Prop}-valued predicate. Encoding:
1870	    \texttt{\(\forall\) \(\eta\) : X, Order.coheight \(\eta\) = 1 \(\to\) \(\eta\) \(\in\)
1871	    f.domain} (matching the \texttt{Order.coheight} idiom of
1872	    \cref{def:prime_divisor}).
1873	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} ---
1874	    \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, a lemma (or instance) producing
1875	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing (Scheme.LocalRing.at\ X \(\eta\))} under the
1876	    typeclass set \texttt{[Smooth (\(\Spec \bar k\)) X] [IsIntegral X]} and the
1877	    hypothesis \texttt{Order.coheight \(\eta\) = 1}.
1878	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_of\_codimOneFree\_of\_smooth}
1879	    --- \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, the existence-of-extension theorem. Signature
1880	    (informal): given a smooth variety \(X\), a complete variety \(Y\), and
1881	    \(f\)~codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free, produce a unique regular morphism
1882	    \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\) extending \(f\). Consumes
1883	    \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} from
1884	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} as a Mathlib-style import.
1885	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy\_pure\_codim\_one\_into\_grpScheme}
1886	    --- \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}. Signature (informal): given a smooth
1887	    variety \(X\), a group scheme \(G\) over \(\bar k\) that is a variety (smooth, finite
1888	    type, separated), and \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) rational, conclude that
1889	    \(Z(f)\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
1890	  \item % NOTE (iter-184 review): the declaration name `extend\_iff\_order\_nonneg`
1891	        % was RETIRED in iter-179 (renamed to
1892	        % `mem\_domain\_iff\_exists\_partialMap\_through\_point`; per the
1893	        % iter-179 review of auditor 178B). The new declaration is at
1894	        % AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean:492.
1895	        % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the lightweight blueprint home for the
1896	        % reshuffle now exists at \texttt{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle};
1897	        % the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 `ord_W \geq 0` form remains at
1898	        % \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} (unpinned until the
1899	        % `Scheme.RationalMap` -> function-field pullback machinery lands).
1900	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain\_iff\_exists\_partialMap\_through\_point}
1901	    --- the iter-179 definitional reshuffle pinned at
1902	    \texttt{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}. The substantive
1903	    Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole'' Weil-divisor reformulation lives
1904	    at \texttt{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} (unpinned at the chapter level;
1905	    the iter-179 detach \texttt{\% NOTE} on that theorem block explains the
1906	    reshuffle). Re-uses
1907	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order} from
1908	    \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} (the order map at a prime divisor).
1909	\end{enumerate}
1910	
1911	\paragraph{Mathlib readiness audit.} At commit \texttt{b80f227} the prover should
1912	verify:
1913	\begin{itemize}
1914	  \item \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain} is shipped at
1915	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; its complement
1916	    \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} is a one-line definition.
1917	  \item The valuative criterion of properness in Mathlib form is
1918	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.valuative\_criterion} (or analogous); the
1919	    prover invokes this in Step~1 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof.
1920	  \item % NOTE (iter-184 review): the previous claim that Mathlib's
1921	        % \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth} ``bundles the regular-local-rings
1922	        % hypothesis'' was FALSE at b80f227 — empirically verified by
1923	        % lean-vs-blueprint-checker iter184-codimone. The implication
1924	        % \texttt{Smooth X.hom + [IsAlgClosed kbar] $\Rightarrow$ IsRegularLocalRing
1925	        % (X.left.presheaf.stalk z)} is a Mathlib GAP (Stacks tag 00TT, the
1926	        % Jacobian-criterion direction), not a bundled instance. The
1927	        % \texttt{Krull-dim} half of \texttt{lem:smooth\_codim\_one\_dvr} now
1928	        % closes axiom-clean via \texttt{Scheme.ringKrullDim\_stalk\_eq\_coheight}
1929	        % (iter-183 \texttt{Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}) + \texttt{exact\_mod\_cast};
1930	        % the \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half remains as a typed sorry pending
1931	        % Stacks 00TT formalisation (iter-185+ blueprint-expansion required).
1932	        % Once \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} is in scope, the closure
1933	        % ``regular local + Krull dim 1 = DVR'' goes via
1934	        % \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE.out 0 4} (the iter-178
1935	        % closure pattern).
1936	        The DVR-at-codim-1 lemma reduces to ``regular local of dim 1 = DVR''
1937	    (\texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing.TFAE.out 0 4}; the iter-178 closure pattern).
1938	    The bridge \texttt{Smooth X.hom + [IsAlgClosed kbar] $\Rightarrow$
1939	    IsRegularLocalRing (stalk z)} is the Stacks 00TT gap — see the iter-184 note above.
1940	  \item Mathlib's product-of-smooth-is-smooth instance,
1941	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.comp\_pullback} / equivalent, is needed in
1942	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}'s Sub-step~4 to apply the Weil-divisor
1943	    machinery to \(X \times X\).
1944	  \item The local-cohomology vanishing in Step~2 of
1945	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof
1946	    (\(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) at a
1947	    depth-\(\geq 2\) point) is a substantial Mathlib gap as of \texttt{b80f227}; the
1948	    prover's options are (i) prove the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property as a local
1949	    lemma using the \texttt{Module.depth} / Auslander--Buchsbaum machinery of
1950	    \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}, or (ii) reformulate
1951	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} in the weaker ``surface'' form
1952	    (where \(X\) has \(\dim 2\), so codim-\(\geq 2\) means closed points only and the
1953	    extension is the classical Hartshorne~III.7.1 ``smooth-surface point removal''
1954	    argument). For the consumer \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} only
1955	    the surface case is needed in practice (the symmetric product \(C^{(g)}\) is
1956	    smooth, and the Abel--Jacobi map's intermediate-step indeterminacy lives on a
1957	    smooth surface inside \(C^{(g)} \times C^{(g)}\)).
1958	\end{itemize}
1959	
1960	No new core axiom is required. The proof of \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is
1961	purely Weil-divisor manipulation on a smooth variety and is the cleanest part of
1962	the chapter; the substance of the Lean engineering is in
1963	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s proof, both halves of which rest on Mathlib API
1964	gaps the prover must either fill in or work around.
1965	
1966	\section{Out of scope}
1967	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1968	
1969	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1970	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1971	scope:
1972	\begin{itemize}
1973	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1974	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1975	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1976	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1977	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1978	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1979	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1980	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1981	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1982	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1983	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1984	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1985	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1986	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1987	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1988	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1989	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1990	    (\texttt{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1991	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1992	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1993	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1994	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1995	    \texttt{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1996	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1997	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1998	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1999	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
2000	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
2001	    introduced.
2002	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
2003	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
2004	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
2005	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
2006	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
2007	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
2008	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
2009	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
2010	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
2011	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
2012	\end{itemize}

122	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
123	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
124	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
125	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
126	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
127	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
128	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
129	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
130	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
131	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
132	\end{definition}
133	
134	
135	\begin{definition}
136	
137	
138	\leanok
139	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
140	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
142	  \uses{def:indeterminacy_locus, def:prime_divisor}
143	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
144	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
145	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
146	  % PDF page 22).
147	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
148	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
149	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
150	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
151	  conclusion).}
152	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
153	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
154	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
155	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
156	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
157	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
158	
159	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
160	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
161	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
162	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
163	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
164	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
165	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
166	  is empty) as a single line.
167	
168	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
169	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
170	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
171	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
172	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
173	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), \(\eta \in
174	  \texttt{f.domain}\). The encoding matches the standing \(\mathrm{Order.coheight}\)
175	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
176	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
180	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
181	
182	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
183	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
184	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
185	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
186	
187	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
188	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
189	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
190	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
191	
192	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
193	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
194	
195	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
196	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
197	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a

\subsection{K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate}
\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}

The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) is assembled from several
substrate results: smoothness gives flatness at a stalk and, locally, a
standard-smooth presentation; the scheme-to-algebra bridge and the freeness of
\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\) then reduce the
regularity claim to a freeness-plus-rank computation on K\"ahler differentials,
transported through localisation at the prime defining the stalk. The two
localisation helpers below --- transport of freeness, and matching of rank under
localisation --- supply this reduction. The Krull-dimension and cotangent inputs
are collected in \cref{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}, and the regularity
conclusion at an arbitrary prime is \cref{thm:standard_smooth_regular_at_prime}.

The substrate lemmas are recorded below, feeding
\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}.

\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain (iter-198)}
\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}

Stage 6 is the residual gap inside
\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}'s six-stage chain: given Stages
1--5b (a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart
\(R \to S\) at a point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\)
and \(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\)), one must conclude that
\(S_\mathfrak q\) is a regular local ring. The Stacks proof of tag
\texttt{00TT} ultimately reduces to two ingredients which Mathlib does
not yet ship at commit \texttt{b80f227}: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}, in the algebra
chapter as Lemma~\texttt{lemma-dimension-at-a-point-finite-type-field}),
and (6.B) the cotangent / K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field
base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, in the algebra chapter as
Lemma~\texttt{lemma-differential-seq} plus the separable-residue-field
refinement of Lemma~\texttt{lemma-separable-smooth}). The two
ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
which is the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity that
Mathlib's \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.iff\_finrank\_cotangentSpace}
already exposes. The next three lemmas package the three sub-gaps as
named iter-199+ prover targets.

\subsection{Stage 6 sub-decomposition: 00OE + 02JK chain}
\label{subsec:stage6_subgap_decomposition}

Given a standard-smooth presentation of an affine chart \(R \to S\) at a
point \(z\), with \(\Omega_{S/R}\) free of rank \(n\) and
\(\Omega_{S_\mathfrak q / R}\) free of rank \(n\) on the stalk
\(S_\mathfrak q = \mathcal O_{X, z}\), one concludes that \(S_\mathfrak q\)
is a regular local ring from two ingredients: (6.A) the smooth-algebra
Krull-dimension formula (Stacks tag \texttt{00OE}), and (6.B) the cotangent /
K\"ahler-differential bridge over a field base (Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}). The
two ingredients combine (6.C) into the regularity conclusion via
\(\dim S_\mathfrak q = \mathrm{rank}_{S_\mathfrak q}\Omega_{S_\mathfrak
q / R} = \dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \mathfrak m / \mathfrak m^2\),
the cotangent-space characterisation of regularity. The next three lemmas
record these three ingredients.

1262	  \(\mathrm{coheight}\,z = 1\) (a codimension-one point). Then the maximal ideal of the
1263	  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X,z}\) is principal and nonzero.
1264	\end{lemma}
1265	
1266	\begin{proof}
1267	  Proved directly in Lean (modulo the named Stacks 00TT regularity gap of
1268	  \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}): smoothness gives a regular local stalk via
1269	  \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}; the coheight bridge
1270	  \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} gives Krull dimension \(1\); the
1271	  cotangent-space characterisation of regularity then forces
1272	  \(\dim_\kappa \mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2 = 1\), i.e.\ a principal maximal ideal, which
1273	  is nonzero since the stalk is not a field. This is the geometric core consumed by
1274	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}.
1275	\end{proof}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}
1278	
1279	
1280	\leanok
1281	  [Smooth \(\Rightarrow\) DVR at every codim-\(1\) point]
1282	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
1283	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
1284	  \uses{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring, thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight,
1285	        lem:smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_principal}
1286	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
1287	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
1288	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
1289	  % PDF page 147).
1290	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
1291	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
1292	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
1293	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
1294	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
1295	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the
1296	  % local rings are regular, since they are localizations of the local rings of
1297	  % closed points."
1298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
1299	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
1300	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\)."
1301	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of regular in codimension one;
1302	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
1303	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
1304	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
1305	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
1306	\end{lemma}
1307	
1308	\begin{proof}
1309	
1310	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
1311	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
1312	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
1313	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
1314	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
1315	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
1316	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
1317	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
1318	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
1319	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
1320	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).
1321	
1322	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
1323	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} is the
1324	  instance/lemma stating that \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a Mathlib
1325	  \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing} under the typeclass set
1326	  \texttt{[Smooth\,(\dots)\,X.hom]\ [IsIntegral X.left]} and the hypothesis
1327	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ \(\eta\) = 1}. The fraction-field identification with
1328	  \texttt{X.functionField} is a downstream named instance
1329	  \texttt{Scheme.fractionRing\_localRing\_eq\_functionField} (or whatever Mathlib name
1330	  the prover chooses at commit \texttt{b80f227}). The regular-local-ring-of-dimension-1
1331	  \(\Leftrightarrow\) DVR equivalence is shipped in Mathlib as
1332	  \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing.isDiscreteValuationRing\_of\_dim\_one} (or analogous);
1333	  the prover re-exports it rather than re-proving.
1334	\end{proof}
